title: Camera Coverage Area Guide
summary: Understand how horizontal FOV, vertical FOV, target distance, and overlap reserve shape the practical coverage footprint of a camera.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the approximate rectangular coverage area at a target plane and subtracts overlap reserve so the result reflects usable planning coverage rather than perfect edge-to-edge math.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when comparing camera placement options, estimating how much floor or scene area one view can cover, or planning early camera counts before detailed spacing work.
bullets: Early design planning.
Camera placement review.
Expansion or retrofit validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical physical security planning terms.

items.label: Horizontal FOV (deg)
items.description: The side-to-side viewing angle of the camera/lens at the selected target distance.
items.whyItMatters: Horizontal FOV controls coverage width and strongly affects detail density.
items.commonMistake: Treating horizontal coverage as usable all the way to the image edge without overlap or distortion reserve.

links.label: Check detail after coverage area
links.note: A large coverage area can still be too low-detail for the goal.

items.label: Vertical FOV (deg)
items.description: The top-to-bottom viewing angle of the camera/lens.
items.whyItMatters: Vertical FOV helps estimate coverage depth/height at the target plane and whether the scene fits vertically.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring vertical angle until the camera misses the floor, doorway, or high target area.

items.label: Distance to Target Plane (ft)
items.description: The planning distance from the camera to the plane where coverage is being estimated.
items.whyItMatters: Coverage width and height expand with distance, while usable detail usually decreases.
items.commonMistake: Using the room depth instead of the actual plane where coverage matters.

items.label: Overlap Reserve (%)
items.description: The percentage of the calculated area held back for overlap, aiming tolerance, distortion, or edge softness.
items.whyItMatters: Overlap reserve prevents the design from relying on perfect edge alignment between views.
items.commonMistake: Counting every calculated square foot as usable without allowing for overlap or field adjustment.

links.label: Use spacing when cameras repeat along a path
links.note: Camera Spacing converts overlap strategy into placement distance.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the estimated usable area has reasonable coverage for planning. Watch means overlap, detail, or camera count should be reviewed. Risk means the selected view may not cover enough usable area or may be too stretched for practical detail.
bullets: Area coverage is useful for early planning but does not prove recognition detail.
Overlap reserve should increase when coverage gaps are unacceptable.
Use camera spacing or blind spot checks when multiple cameras must work together.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most physical security planning problems come from treating camera coverage, recognition detail, mounting position, and lighting as separate decisions. In the field, those choices interact: a wider view lowers pixel density, a higher mount changes target geometry, and poor lighting can erase detail that looked acceptable on paper.
bullets: Sizing a camera view only by how wide the scene looks, without checking target detail.
Using clean daytime assumptions for a scene that must also work at night, in glare, or under motion.
Ignoring overlap, obstructions, mounting limits, and the actual target plane.
Treating calculated values as final design approval instead of documented planning assumptions.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: These guides explain the planning logic behind the tool. They do not replace manufacturer documentation, site survey, camera testing, client acceptance criteria, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera model, lens, sensor, encoder settings, and mounting location.
Confirm lighting, reflections, obstructions, weather, and target movement before relying on a design.
Use calculated outputs as design evidence and review notes, not as a substitute for field validation.
Leave practical margin when the scene is important, difficult, or expected to change.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, on-site testing, legal review, privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Document assumptions before implementation.
Review the design with the owner, integrator, manufacturer, and applicable authority when required.
Test representative views before treating a camera placement as final.
